---
title: Working with QGIS Projects
---

## What is a QGIS project?

A QGIS project is the file that saves your work in QGIS.
It remembers:

- the layers you have open
- the map view and zoom level
- styles and colours for your map
- project settings such as units and coordinate system

A project file usually ends in `.qgz` or `.qgs`.

## Open a sample project

To open the sample project:
1. In QGIS, choose **Project > Open**.
2. Find the `.qgz` or `.qgs` file you want.
3. Double click the file and let QGIS load the map.

You can also open a project by double-clicking the project file in your file manager or by choosing it from the **Recent Projects** list on the QGIS start screen.

![QGIS Start Screen](../figs/GISI/Working_with_QGIS_Projects/Opening_ Screen.png)

::: {.callout-tip title="Your Turn"}
Open the demo project provided for this course. The project file and data are located in the Demo_project folder. The project file is named `demo_project.qgz`. Open this file in QGIS to explore the layers and settings.
:::

## Explore the opened project

Once the project is open, start by moving around the map and looking at the main tools.

### Move the map

- Use the **Pan tool** (hand icon) to drag the map.
- Use **Zoom In** and **Zoom Out** (magnifying glass icons) to change the scale.
- You can also use the mouse scroll wheel to zoom in and out.

::: {.callout-tip title="Your Turn"}
1. Find an area that looks interesting.
2. Pan the map and zoom in and out.
3. Can you tell where you are on the map?
:::

### Work with layers

The map is made of layers. Each layer contains different information.

- Layers near the top appear above others.
- Turning layers on and off changes what you see.
- Changing the layer order can reveal or hide details.

::: {.callout-tip title="Your Turn"}
4. What layers do you see in the Layers Panel?
5. Try moving a layer up or down.
6. Turn a layer off, then turn it back on.
:::

## Project properties and CRS

QGIS saves important settings for the current project. Two key settings are:

- **Units** (for example meters, feet, or degrees)
- **Coordinate Reference System (CRS)**, which tells us how the map fits the real world

Open these settings at **Project > Properties** or press **Ctrl+Shift+P**. Explore the **General** and **CRS** tabs.

![QGIS Project Properties](../figs/GISI/Working_with_QGIS_Projects/Project _Properties.png)

### Check the project details

- What CRS is the project using?
- What units are shown?
- Can you see the project title and author fields?

::: {.callout-tip title="Your Turn"}
Open Project Properties and note the CRS and units. If you are working on Wurundjeri Country, the project may use GDA2020 / MGA Zone 55 (EPSG:7855) or VicGrid (EPSG:7899).
:::

## Layer properties

Each layer also has its own settings.

To view them:

- double-click a layer in the Layers Panel, or
- right-click a layer and choose **Properties**.

![QGIS Layer Properties](../figs/GISI/Working_with_QGIS_Projects/Layer_Properties.png)

Layer properties show details like the layer name, style, and coordinate system.

## Find information about your data

QGIS can tell you about a specific point or object on the map.

![QGIS Information Icon](../figs/GISI/Working_with_QGIS_Projects/Information.PNG)

### Use Identify Features

- Click the blue **Identify Features** icon.
- Click on a point or object on the map.
- Read the results in the **Identify Results** panel.

You can also choose **View > Identify Features** or press **Ctrl+Shift+I**.

### Find coordinates quickly

As you move the mouse over the map, watch the coordinate box in the status bar at the bottom.

- Large numbers like `320500, 5812000` usually mean Eastings/Northings (MGA).
- Smaller numbers like `144.9, -37.8` usually mean Longitude/Latitude.

### Open the attribute table

The attribute table shows the data behind a layer.

- Right-click a layer in the Layers Panel.
- Choose **Open Attribute Table**.
- Or press **F6**.

For more details, see the official QGIS guide:
[QGIS Attribute Table documentation](https://docs.qgis.org/3.44/en/docs/user_manual/working_with_vector/attribute_table.html)

::: {.callout-tip title="Your Turn"}
1. Where is the pan tool?
2. How can you zoom without using the toolbar icons?
3. Use Identify Features on a map point and look at the results.
:::
 
<!-- - How to find the location of a feature with a given attribute value (i.e. using the "Attributes table" tool) -->

<!-- TODO: Add instructions for opening a sample project -->
<!-- - How QGIS links into live documents (i.e. warning not to move source files around or information on how to update source locations if you do need to move things).

- Metadata and crediting.-->
